prevalence.source: National family and nutrition survey syntheses; WHO anemia estimates context
prevalence.trend: slowly decreasing with supplementation programs; remains high

incidence.value: Very common; peak risk in 6-24 months

burden.dalyPerCapita: high population burden due to very high prevalence; impacts development and growth
burden.mortalityRate: Indirect; increases vulnerability to infections and adverse developmental outcomes
burden.source: IHME/GBD anemia context; WHO child nutrition

riskFactors: food insecurity and poor diet diversity
vulnerablePopulations: rural areas with limited access to supplementation
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Hemoglobin below age thresholds with iron studies when available (ferritin with inflammation context); dietary assessment; evaluate parasites and chronic disease contributors

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: variable; anemia screening and supplementation programs exist but coverage is inconsistent
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Tens of millions of children affected
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate; depends on adherence and program continuity

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Primary care and community health worker programs screen and supplement; lab confirmation uneven
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Oral iron supplementation, nutrition counseling, food fortification where available, deworming and infection control; treat underlying causes
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Adherence issues, supply chain interruptions, high baseline malnutrition and infection burden
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Large-scale public programs can reach high-risk children when resourced and sustained
